title: Technical Implementation Addendum
text:
We will use a modular repository structure with separate repos for each major GHG source category, coordinated through an overarching GitHub Project board.


github.com/your-org/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ghg-inventory-project/ # GitHub Project
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ghg-energy/ # IPCC Category 1: Energy
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ghg-ippu/ # IPCC Category 2: Industrial Processes
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ghg-agriculture/ # IPCC Category 3: Agriculture
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ghg-lulucf/ # IPCC Category 4: Land Use
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ghg-waste/ # IPCC Category 5: Waste
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ghg-core/ # Shared utilities & documentation
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ syrinx/ # Internal data harmonization package



Advantages: - Source independence: Teams can work without conflicts - Clear ownership: Each source lead owns their repository - Separate release cycles: Update sources independently - Smaller codebases: Easier to understand and maintain - Specific issues: Source-specific discussions stay organized
Coordination via GitHub Project: - Cross-repo task tracking - Unified timeline and milestones - Dependencies between sectors visible - Overall inventory status dashboard




Each sector repository follows a consistent structure:
ghg-energy/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md # Sector overview & quick start
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ METHODOLOGY.md # Detailed methodology documentation
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ data/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ reference/ # Emission factors, lookups
‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ emission_factors.csv
‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ gwp_values.csv
‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ fuel_classifications.csv
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ activity/ # Processed activity data (git-ignored if large)
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ calculated/ # Results (date-stamped)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ data-raw/ # Original downloads (git-ignored)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ R/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 01_download.R # Data acquisition scripts
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 02_clean.R # Data cleaning and harmonization
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 03_calculate.R # Emission calculations
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 04_validate.R # QA/QC checks
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ functions/ # Helper functions
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ load_data.R
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ calculations.R
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ validation.R
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ testthat/ # Unit tests
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ test_calculations.R
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ outputs/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ reports/ # Generated reports
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ figures/ # Plots and visualizations
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ docs/ # Additional documentation
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ data_dictionary.md
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ changelog.md
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ .github/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ workflows/ # GitHub Actions (CI/CD)
‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ run_tests.yml
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ISSUE_TEMPLATE/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ renv.lock # R package dependencies
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ _targets.R # Targets workflow (optional)



Central repository for shared resources:
ghg-core/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ reference_data/ # Cross-sector reference data
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ipcc_categories.csv # Complete IPCC hierarchy
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ gwp_ar5.csv # Global warming potentials
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ gwp_ar6.csv
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ state_codes.csv
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ region_mappings.csv
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ templates/ # Templates for sector repos
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ repo_template/
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ script_templates/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ documentation/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ methodology_standards.md # Cross-sector standards
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ qaqc_procedures.md
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ naming_conventions.md
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ git_workflow.md
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ R/
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ shared_functions/ # Functions used across sectors
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/



Project Name: U.S. GHG Inventory Development
Board Structure:
Columns:
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Backlog # Future work
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Ready # Prioritized and scoped
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ In Progress # Active development
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Review # Code review / validation
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Done # Completed this sprint
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Blocked # Waiting on dependencies
Issue Labels (cross-repo): - sector:energy, sector:agriculture, etc. - priority:high, priority:medium, priority:low - type:bug, type:feature, type:documentation - status:blocked, status:needs-review - phase:discovery, phase:mvp, phase:expansion

title: Technical Implementation Addendum
text:
R Package Ecosystem

Core Packages

Data Management & Manipulation
# Key packages
library(tidyverse) # Data manipulation, visualization
library(readxl) # Excel file import
library(remotes) # Easy package deployment
library(jsonlite) # Read and create JSON files
library(janitor) # Data cleaning utilities
library(rvest) # Webscraping
library(httr) # Work with HMTL data

 # Our custom package
library(syrinx) # Internal data harmonization and data cleaning


Workflow & Reproducibility
library(here) # Path management
library(ellmer) # Leverage LLMs via API
library(targets) # Workflow orchestration (optional Phase 2)
library(renv) # Dependency management
library(usethis) # Development utilities


Quality Assurance
library(testthat) # Unit testing
library(pointblank) # Data validation
library(assertr) # Data quality checks
library(validate) # Rule-based validation


Documentation & Reporting
library(quarto) # Document generation
library(knitr) # Report generation
library(kableExtra) # Table formatting
library(gt) # Publication-ready tables
library(roxygen2) # Automated documentation


Visualization
library(ggplot2) # Core plotting
library(patchwork) # Compose multiple plots
library(scales) # Scale functions
library(plotly) # Interactive plots (optional)


Spatial Data (for LULUCF)
library(sf) # Spatial data handling
library(terra) # Raster data
library(mapview) # Interactive maps


Database (Future Phase)
library(DBI) # Database interface
library(duckdb) # DuckDB connection (when needed)



The syrinx Package
Purpose: Internal package for standardizing data cleaning and harmonization across all sectors.
Key Functions:
# Data harmonization and validation
syrinx::pre_clean() # Convenience function; wraps several data cleaning operations
syrinx::use_dictionary() # Consult dictionary to validate input data
syrinx::llm_dictionary() # LLM-generated corrections for invalid data

# Common transformations
syrinx::apply_metadata_labels() # Label dataframe columns 
syrinx::convert_units_in() # Convert input data to canonical units
syrinx::convert_units_out() # Convert output data to desired units
Package Structure:
syrinx/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ DESCRIPTION
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ NAMESPACE
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ R/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ harmonize.R
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ transform.R
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ utils.R
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ data/ # Built-in reference data
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ standard_units.rda
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ standard_columns.rda
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ testthat/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ man/ # Documentation
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ vignettes/ # Usage examples
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ getting_started.Rmd
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ validation_guide.Rmd
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md
Installation:
# From internal repository
remotes::install_github("joewcorra/syrinx")

# Or if using private GitHub
remotes::install_github("joewcorra/syrinx", 
                        auth_token = Sys.getenv("GITHUB_PAT"))


Package Management Strategy
Using renv for Reproducibility:
Each sector repository includes renv.lock to freeze package versions:
# Initialize renv in a new sector repo
renv::init()

# Install packages
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "here", "testthat"))
remotes::install_github("joewcorra/syrinx")

# Snapshot current state
renv::snapshot()

# Others can restore exact environment
renv::restore()

title: Technical Implementation Addendum
text:
Future Decision Points

Phase 1 Decisions (Months 0-6)

1.1 Workflow Orchestration: {targets} vs Scripts
Decision: Whether to implement {targets} pipeline management
Current Approach: Sequential R scripts (01_download.R, 02_clean.R, etc.)
Consider {targets} when: - Scripts become complex with many interdependencies - Rebuilding everything is taking too long - Need fine-grained caching of intermediate results - Multiple people running overlapping analyses
Evaluation Criteria: - Is manual script execution becoming error-prone? - Are we re-running expensive calculations unnecessarily? - Would the learning curve justify the benefits?
Timeline: Re-evaluate after MVP (Month 4)



1.2 Testing Strategy: Depth vs Speed
Decision: How comprehensive should automated testing be?
Current Approach: Basic unit tests for calculation functions
Options: - Minimal: Test core calculations only - Moderate: Add data validation tests - Comprehensive: Include integration tests, benchmark tests
Consider expansion when: - Bugs are discovered in production - Onboarding new team members who break things - Preparing for external review
Timeline: Decide by Month 3 based on bug frequency




Phase 2 Decisions (Months 6-12)

2.1 Database Implementation: DuckDB
Decision: When/whether to migrate from CSVs to DuckDB
Current Approach: Well-organized CSV files
Triggers for migration: - ‚úì Query performance becomes problematic (joins taking >1 minute) - ‚úì File management becomes unwieldy (>100 CSV files) - ‚úì Need for referential integrity is critical - ‚úì Team size grows (>5 active users) - ‚úì Complex analytical queries are frequent
Migration Path:
# Phase 1: Read CSVs (current)
data <- read_csv("data/activity/electricity.csv")

# Phase 2: Add DuckDB option
con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), "ghg_inventory.duckdb")
data <- dbReadTable(con, "electricity")

# Functions remain unchanged
calculate_emissions(data, emission_factors)
Implementation Steps: 1. Pilot DuckDB with one sector 2. Benchmark performance vs CSVs 3. Evaluate team comfort level 4. Decide to expand or revert
Timeline: Evaluate Month 8, implement Month 9-10 if approved



2.2 Centralized Data Acquisition Hub
Decision: Build automated data download/update system
Current Approach: Manual downloads, sector-by-sector
Future Vision:
ghg-data-hub/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ R/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ download_eia.R # EIA API wrapper
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ download_epa.R # EPA GHGRP
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ download_usda.R # USDA NASS
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ schedule_updates.R # Automated scheduling
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ data/
‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ raw/ # Downloaded files
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ metadata/ # Download logs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ logs/
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ download_log.csv # Audit trail
Features: - Centralized data source definitions - Automated update checks - Version control of raw data - Notification system for new data - Consistent metadata tracking
Triggers for implementation: - ‚úì Manual downloads becoming bottleneck - ‚úì Missing data updates causing delays - ‚úì Multiple sectors using same data sources - ‚úì Need for systematic data versioning
Dependencies: - API access established with agencies - Data source landscape well-understood - Team agreement on update frequency
Timeline: Design Month 7, pilot Month 10-12



2.3 Web-Based Data Portal
Decision: Create public-facing data access system
Options: - Shiny Dashboard: Interactive exploration - API: Programmatic access - Static Website: Documentation + download links - All of the above: Comprehensive platform
Consider when: - Inventory is stable and validated - External users requesting data access - Need to demonstrate transparency - Ready for public scrutiny
Timeline: Not before Month 12 (Phase 3+)




Phase 3+ Decisions (Year 2+)

3.1 Advanced Uncertainty Quantification
Decision: Implement Monte Carlo simulation
Current Approach: Use published uncertainty ranges from EPA
Enhanced Approach: - Probability distributions for emission factors - Correlation structures between parameters - Propagation through calculations - Full uncertainty analysis per IPCC guidelines
Package: {mc2d} or {propagate}
Triggers: - Required for peer review or publication - IPCC reporting requirements - Stakeholder requests for uncertainty bounds

title: Technical Implementation Addendum
text:
Decision-Making Framework

When to Add Complexity
Ask these questions:

Does the current simple approach actually cause problems?

If no ‚Üí Don‚Äôt add complexity
If yes ‚Üí Continue evaluation

What‚Äôs the cost of the status quo?

Time wasted?
Errors introduced?
Opportunities missed?

What‚Äôs the cost of the new approach?

Learning curve?
Maintenance burden?
Technical debt?

Who benefits and how much?

Entire team or one person?
Daily impact or occasional?
Critical or nice-to-have?

Is it reversible?

Can we go back if it doesn‚Äôt work?
What‚Äôs locked in?

Do we have capacity?

Time to implement properly?
Skills on the team?
Bandwidth to maintain?




Decision Authority
Tech decisions by data system developer: - R package choices - Code organization - Git workflow details - Development tools
Collaborative decisions: - Database implementation - Major workflow changes - Data access/sharing policies - Public-facing features
Sector lead decisions: - Sector-specific methodologies - Data source selections - QA/QC thresholds - Reporting priorities

title: Technical Implementation Addendum
text:
Review Milestones
Month 3 Review: - Is CSV approach working? - Is syrinx package valuable? - Are GitHub repos effective? - Need to adjust course?
Month 6 Review: - Ready for DuckDB evaluation? - Need centralized data hub? - Testing strategy adequate? - Workflow orchestration needed?
Month 12 Review: - System meeting goals? - What future capabilities to prioritize? - Resource needs for Year 2? - External engagement strategy?

title: Technical Implementation Addendum
text:
Appendix: Key Package Documentation

Essential Reading
{tidyverse}: https://www.tidyverse.org/
{targets}: https://books.ropensci.org/targets/
{renv}: https://rstudio.github.io/renv/
{testthat}: https://testthat.r-lib.org/
{pointblank}: https://rich-iannone.github.io/pointblank/
{DuckDB}: https://duckdb.org/docs/api/r
{quarto}: https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html


Internal Documentation
{syrinx} package: [Link to internal docs when available]
GitHub organization: [Your organization link]
Team wiki: [Link to wiki]

This addendum is a living document and will be updated as decisions are made and the system evolves.

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
text:
With the discontinuation of the official EPA inventory compilation, we face the challenge of maintaining continuity in U.S. emissions tracking while seizing the opportunity to rebuild our system using modern practices that can serve the climate community for decades.
As your data system developer, my role is to create infrastructure that empowers each source lead to do their best work while ensuring the overall inventory is coherent, reproducible, and maintainable.

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
text:
With the discontinuation of the official EPA inventory compilation, we face the challenge of maintaining continuity in U.S. emissions tracking while seizing the opportunity to rebuild our system using modern practices that can serve the climate community for decades.
As your data system developer, my role is to create infrastructure that empowers each source lead to do their best work while ensuring the overall inventory is coherent, reproducible, and maintainable.

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
text:
Four Guiding Principles



ü°Ü Infrastructure First


Minimize Complexity, Maximize Utility

A well-designed data system from day one prevents technical debt, ensures scalability and reproducibility, and saves time in the long run. Good infrastructure accelerates work; bad infrastructure creates endless friction.



üë• User-Centered Design


Learn from the Source Leads

Built for you, not imposed on you. Focus on your work, not data housekeeping and chasing errors in spreadsheets. Your domain expertise will guide all technical implementation decisions.





üîÑ Iterative Development


Build, Learn, Improve

Start simple, deploy quickly, gather feedback continuously, and improve based on real-world use. Add new solutions when needed, discard what doesn‚Äôt work, make room for new ideas. Every tool or process must answer: Does this make compilation easier, faster, more accurate, or more transparent?



üõ°Ô∏è Transparency & Reproducibility


Building Resilience Through Openness

Every step of data retrieval, transformation, aggregation, and calculation will be documented and traceable, building a foundation for future improvements. Our goal is not next year‚Äôs Inventory‚Äìit‚Äôs every Inventory, every year.

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
text:
Development Roadmap

Phase 1 Discovery (Weeks 1-TBD) ‚Äî Meet with leads, document workflows, set priorities


Phase 2 Pilot (Months TBD) ‚Äî Build pilot sector, establish patterns, deliver working results


Phase 3 Expansion (Months TBD) ‚Äî Extend to additional sectors, refine based on lessons learned


Phase 4 Operations (Ongoing) ‚Äî Maintain, improve, adapt, and ensure long-term sustainability

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
text:
Technical Approach: Foundations



üóÑÔ∏è Our Foundation


R + {tidyverse} for calculations and data transformation


Organized CSVs for transparent data storage


Git for version control and collaboration




üéØ Start Simple


Our data may not require need complex databases


Simple joins (2‚Äì4 tables) work great with R‚Äôs tidyverse tools


Add complexity only as needed.



Key Insight: We‚Äôre optimizing for maintainability, transparency, and resilience. Modern R handles your data volumes easily, and CSV files ensure that anyone can inspect, verify, and trust our inputs.

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
text:
Current Toolkit and Future Capabilities



Available Now

Centralized communication and issue resolution via GitHub
Data pipelines for workflow management
Coded data pulls via API and webscraping
Scheduled data retrieval
Automated metadata labeling
Integrated data dictionary for data cleaning
LLM-driven data harmonization tool



In Development

Automatic unit conversion
‚ÄúUnitless‚Äù internal calculations
Automated code and workflow documentation
Automated figure and table generation
Scheduled, automated Quarto reporting



Future Options

Column-oriented relational database system
Centralized Data Hub to manage all data storage and retrieval
Interactive data exploration dashboards
Automated QA/QC tools
Cloud deployment
Advanced modeling including Monte Carlo simulations
GIS analysis tools

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
section: What I Will Need From the Team
text:
What I Will Need From the Team

Time for initial discovery conversations
Honesty about what works and what doesn‚Äôt in your current workflow
Patience as we iterate toward better solutions
Feedback when something isn‚Äôt working as expected
Ideas about what would make your work easier

title: Building a Modern U.S. GHG Inventory Data System
section: What You Can Expect From Me
text:
What You Can Expect From Me

Responsiveness to your needs and concerns
Transparency about technical decisions and tradeoffs
Documentation so the system is never a black box
Training to ensure everyone can use the tools effectively
Flexibility to adapt as we learn what works
Accountability for delivering a system that actually helps

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Long-Range Vision ‚Ä¢ 2-3 Years



This document outlines an ambitious vision for transforming the U.S. GHG Inventory system into a global capacity-building platform using large language models.

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
The Big Idea
Transform the U.S. GHG Inventory system into a global capacity-building platform that uses large language models to generate customized, coded inventory systems for any country based on their specific data availability, technical capacity, and reporting needs.
The Innovation: Use LLMs not to understand GHG science, but as a code generation and customization engine that assembles validated templates into country-specific inventory systems.

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
The Three-Step Framework

Step 1: Distill to Elements üìö
Break down each inventory compilation step into its most fundamental, atomic components:

Data acquisition patterns - How we get activity data
Calculation structures - Mathematical operations and logic
Validation logic - QA/QC checks and thresholds
IPCC tier decision trees - When to use Tier 1 vs.¬Ý2 vs.¬Ý3
Uncertainty propagation - How errors compound through calculations

The key: Every decision, every calculation, every check is decomposed into its simplest form and documented.



Step 2: Build Templates üíª
Construct parameterized code templates for every possible inventory component:

Sector-specific modules - Energy, transport, agriculture, etc.
Data source adapters - EIA, EPA GHGRP, USDA, etc.
Tier 1/2/3 implementations - Multiple methodological approaches
QA/QC procedures - Standardized validation routines
Reporting formats - UNFCCC-compliant outputs

The key: Templates are validated, tested, and proven to work. They‚Äôre not theoretical‚Äîthey‚Äôre extracted from our working U.S. system.



Step 3: LLM Generation ‚ö°
Use LLM to assemble customized inventory systems based on country context:

Data availability assessment - What data does the country have?
Appropriate tier selection - Match methodology to capacity
Code composition & adaptation - Assemble the right templates
Documentation generation - Explain how everything works
Training material creation - Help users understand the system

The key: The LLM doesn‚Äôt invent‚Äîit selects, combines, and customizes pre-validated components.

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
section: Example: Country X Builds Their Inventory
text:
Example: Country X Builds Their Inventory


Input üåç
Country X describes their situation: - Limited industrial data - Good electricity statistics
- Agricultural focus - 3 technical staff members - Need Tier 1/2 methods

LLM Analysis ü§ñ
System assesses capabilities and recommends: - Tier 1 for industry (limited data) - Tier 2 for energy (good statistics) - Prioritize agriculture sector - Simplified workflows for small team




Code Generation üíæ
LLM assembles custom R package: - Appropriate templates selected - Data connectors configured - Workflows simplified - Documentation customized

Output ‚úÖ
Country X receives: - Working code for their context - Documentation in their language - Training materials - Validation tools - Support for updates

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Why This Matters

Global Impact üåç

Democratize GHG inventory capacity - Make it accessible to all countries
Reduce barriers to UNFCCC compliance - Lower technical and financial hurdles
Enable data-driven climate policy globally - Better data ‚Üí Better decisions
Standardize methodologies - While respecting local context and needs



Technical Innovation üí°

LLM as code generation engine - Novel application of AI to climate tools
Automated documentation & training - Scale capacity building efforts
Continuous improvement - Learn from global usage patterns
Quality control through templates - Validated code, not hallucinations

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Critical Challenges to Address






‚öÝÔ∏è Validation & Quality Control



LLM-generated code must be rigorously validated. Human expertise remains essential for methodology review and results verification.








‚öÝÔ∏è Methodological Soundness



IPCC guidelines are complex. Templates must enforce correct methodology and prevent shortcuts that compromise accuracy.








‚öÝÔ∏è Data Source Variability



Every country has unique data landscapes. Adapters must be flexible yet robust enough to handle diverse formats and quality levels.








‚öÝÔ∏è Capacity Building Beyond Code



Code alone isn‚Äôt enough. Countries need training, institutional development, and ongoing support to maintain their systems.

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Roadmap to Reality






Critical Foundation



We can‚Äôt teach other countries to build good inventory systems until we‚Äôve proven our own approach works. Year 1 is about building U.S. infrastructure right‚Äîthat foundation becomes the template for global capacity building.


Year 1: Build & Validate U.S. System - Establish robust data infrastructure - Document every decision and pattern - Create reusable templates - Prove reproducibility and transparency
Year 2: Extract & Pilot - Extract templates from U.S. system - Develop LLM integration - Pilot with 2-3 partner countries - Iterate based on real-world feedback
Year 3: Refine & Expand - Refine system based on lessons learned - Build partnership network - Prepare for broader deployment - Engage with UNFCCC/development organizations
Year 4+: Scale Globally - Deploy to additional countries - Continuous improvement cycle - Establish as international standard - Support long-term maintenance

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Potential Partners & Funders
International Organizations: - UNFCCC Secretariat (Enhanced Transparency Framework) - Global Environment Facility (CBIT funding) - UNEP (Transparency programs) - World Bank / Regional Development Banks
Bilateral Aid: - U.S. State Department (climate finance) - USAID (development assistance) - European bilateral agencies
Foundations: - Climate foundations focused on transparency - Technology for development funders
Technical Partners: - Greenhouse Gas Management Institute - World Resources Institute - Academic institutions

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
The Demand Is Real
Recent research and UNFCCC reporting show:

More than half of developing countries struggle to report emissions reliably
Enhanced Transparency Framework (2024) creates urgent need for capacity
80 countries have sought CBIT funding, but gaps remain
Common challenges: Data systems, institutional capacity, technical expertise

This innovation addresses a documented, urgent, global need with significant funding already allocated.

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Key Success Factors
For this vision to succeed, we need:

‚úÖ Solid U.S. foundation - Can‚Äôt export what we haven‚Äôt proven works
‚úÖ Well-documented templates - Every decision and pattern captured
‚úÖ Rigorous validation - Quality control is non-negotiable
‚úÖ Partnership development - Work with international organizations
‚úÖ Sustained funding - Multi-year commitment required
‚úÖ User feedback loops - Learn from real-world deployment

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Vision Statement






The Opportunity



‚ÄúWhat if the best way to help countries build GHG inventories isn‚Äôt to give them data‚Äîbut to give them the system that generates their own custom inventory from their own data?‚Äù
This isn‚Äôt just about technology. It‚Äôs about democratizing climate transparency and enabling every country to participate meaningfully in global climate action.

title: AI-Powered Global GHG Inventory Capacity Building
text:
Next Steps
If this vision resonates:

Focus on Year 1 - Build the U.S. system right
Document everything - Capture patterns for future templates
Engage stakeholders - Start conversations with potential partners
Secure funding - Identify resources for multi-year effort
Build expertise - Develop LLM integration capabilities

This is a 3-5 year journey, but it starts with building our own foundation correctly.

For questions or to discuss this vision further, please reach out to the project team.
